query: can agy replace antidiuretic hormone in humans

retrieved_docs.title: Hypopituitarism -- Treatment / Management -- ADH deficit
retrieved_docs.content: ADH replacement with intranasal or oral desmopressin, a synthetic analog of vasopressin, aids in stabilizing the water balance and addressing polyuria in patients. Maintaining sodium levels within the normal range is essential. Monitoring urine-specific gravity and osmolality helps validate the effectiveness of ADH replacement and ensures appropriate water regulation. These measurements are crucial indicators to confirm the efficacy of the treatment regimen.

retrieved_docs.title: Syndrome of Inappropriate Antidiuretic Hormone Secretion -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Patients presenting with severe symptoms such as seizures, confusion, or delirium need urgent initial correction with hypertonic saline infusion for the first few hours rather than just water restriction. A 100 mL bolus of 3% hypertonic saline is given in the first 3 to 4 hours, and sodium levels are measured within 2 to 3 hours so that further doses can be adjusted to avoid correcting too rapidly. A rise of 3 to 4 mEq/L within the first few hours in such distressing conditions can be justified. If the patient's mental status does not improve, more boluses of 100 mL hypertonic saline can be given in the same way as above until symptoms get better.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Endocrine Hormones -- Issues of Concern -- Hypothalamus
retrieved_docs.content: Anti-diuretic hormone (ADH) or vasopressin is important in regulating blood volume and electrolyte levels, especially sodium. Its primary function is to regulate serum osmolarity. The ADH levels are lower when the osmolality is below 280 mOsm/kg in a normal individual. This results in water excretion. Conversely, when the plasma osmolality rises above 280 mOsm/kg, the ADH levels increase, resulting in water reabsorption. In addition to osmoreceptor stimulation, volume-sensitive receptors can also trigger ADH release. [3] Volume-sensitive receptors increase ADH if there is a sudden and significant drop in pressure. Small incremental decreases are insufficient to activate ADH – renin and norepinephrine handle these smaller changes instead. [4] [5] ADH increases water retention and raises blood pressure via 2 different receptors. In the distal nephron, V2 receptors help increase water reabsorption by increasing the aquaporin channels in the principal cells of the collecting duct. Increased ADH also stimulates V1 receptors, which increase vascular resistance throughout the body. [6] [7] This topic is discussed in detail. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Syndrome of Inappropriate Antidiuretic Hormone Secretion -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone ADH release (SIADH) is a condition defined by the unsuppressed release of antidiuretic hormone (ADH) from the pituitary gland or nonpituitary sources or its continued action on vasopressin receptors. The condition was first detected in two patients with lung cancer by William Schwartz and Frederic Bartter in 1967. They developed the classic Schwartz and Bartter criteria for the diagnosis of SIADH, which has not changed. SIADH is characterized by impaired water excretion leading to hyponatremia with hypervolemia or euvolemia. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Nocturia -- Treatment / Management -- Antidiuretic hormone therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Desmopressin is very similar to natural vasopressin but contains a change in 2 critical amino acids. This leaves the resulting hormone with substantial antidiuretic effects but eliminates all vasopressor activity, making it the preferred form of antidiuretic medical therapy for nocturia due to nocturnal polyuria. The use of desmopressin is usually associated with a slight increase in daytime diuresis but may lead to hyponatremia in high-risk groups. The lowest effective dose of the medication should be used, especially in older patients, but men tend to need higher dosages than women. This may be because the vasopressin V2 receptor gene is located on the X chromosome in an area relatively protected from inactivation. Overall, desmopressin therapy can reduce nocturia episodes by about 50%, as shown in 2 recent studies of 1045 patients. This effect typically takes 7 days to become clinically evident. When effective, the benefits of desmopressin therapy appear to be long-lasting. [32] Different recommended dosages exist for men and women, as females tend to be more sensitive to desmopressin.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Posterior Pituitary -- Clinical Significance -- Syndrome of Inappropriate Antidiuretic Hormone
retrieved_docs.content: SIADH is a diagnosis of exclusion once all other causes of hyponatremia have been ruled out. Treatment depends on the status of the patient; if asymptomatic, then water restriction is sufficient. If this treatment fails to improve the sodium level, then add lithium or demeclocycline. Both agents antagonize antidiuretic hormone, which will induce diuresis and ultimately correct the sodium level. ADH receptor antagonists like tolvaptan and conivaptan are also approved for the treatment of SIADH. In symptomatic patients, first-line treatment depends on the severity of the symptoms. [3] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Syndrome of Inappropriate Antidiuretic Hormone Secretion -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone ADH release (SIADH) is a condition defined by the unsuppressed release of antidiuretic hormone (ADH) from the pituitary gland or nonpituitary sources or its continued action on vasopressin receptors. The condition was first detected in two patients with lung cancer by William Schwartz and Frederic Bartter in 1967. They developed the classic Schwartz and Bartter criteria for diagnosing SIADH, which has not changed. SIADH is characterized by impaired water excretion leading to hyponatremia with hypervolemia or euvolemia. This activity reviews the causes, presentation, and diagnosis of SIADH and highlights the interprofessional team's role in its management.

retrieved_docs.title: Syndrome of Inappropriate Antidiuretic Hormone Secretion -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The patients with SIADH have a combination of ADH-induced water retention and secondary solute loss. The overall solute loss is more prominent than water retention in patients with chronic SIADH. SIADH treatment involves correction and maintenance of corrected sodium levels and correction of underlying abnormalities such as hypothyroidism or pulmonary or CNS infection. The goal of sodium correction is more than 130 mEq/L.

retrieved_docs.title: Syndrome of Inappropriate Antidiuretic Hormone Secretion -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Outline the benign and malignant causes of the syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone. Describe the blood and urine features of the syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone. Summarize the treatment options for the syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone. Review the importance of improving care coordination among interprofessional team members to improve outcomes for patients affected by inappropriate antidiuretic hormone syndrome. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Syndrome of Inappropriate Antidiuretic Hormone Secretion -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: To correct sodium levels, it should be known that urine osmolality in such patients is usually twice the amount of serum osmolality, i.e., greater than 500 mOsm/kg. So, the fluid needed to correct the sodium levels must have an osmolality that is more than urine osmolality. Isotonic saline may not correct hyponatremia in such patients, or it may even worsen hyponatremia and symptoms. Therefore, a solution with an electrolyte concentration greater than the urine electrolyte concentration must be used.  Three percent hypertonic saline (osmolality 513 mOsm/kg) is used for this purpose in patients with severe symptomatic or resistant hyponatremia. Also, the rate of correction is an important factor. It should not exceed more than 8 mEq/L per 24 hours or 0.5 to 1 mEq/L per hour. More rapid correction can result in osmotic demyelination of the CNS, leading to severe lethal complications such as osmotic demyelination syndrome ("locked-in" syndrome), causing quadriplegia. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Posterior Pituitary -- Function -- Antidiuretic Hormone (ADH)
retrieved_docs.content: ADH, also known as vasopressin, acts as a water-preserving hormone. ADH is released into the blood circulation to vasoconstrict and reabsorbs water from the collecting duct in the kidney to maintain equilibrium intracellularly and extracellularly. There are two regulating receptors in the hypothalamus that sense water depletion and hyperosmolar concentration, the subfornical organ and organum vasculosum. This particular area of the nervous system does not have an intact blood-brain barrier. Therefore, blood products can come into contact with the structures with ease and eventually signal the hypothalamus for ADH secretion. A small concentration is enough to generate water conservation in the renal tubules. The renal tubules are broken down into segments known as the proximal, descending, ascending, distal, and collecting duct. The collecting duct is a highly diluted, ADH-dependent structure in the kidney. The cells in the collecting duct have a 7 pass receptor on the basolateral side where ADH can bind itself. The signal unleashes a power-generating cascade known as cAMP. The Gs protein stimulates adenylyl cyclase, which converts ATP into cAMP. A high CAMP concentration will phosphorylate protein kinase A, opening water channels to allow passage from the luminal side to the basolateral side. The channels are gated vesicles known as aquaporins. The amount of water absorbed from the collecting duct is dependent on the amount of fluid loss. [3] [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Pituitary Gland -- Issues of Concern -- Antidiuretic Hormone (ADH) or Vasopressin
retrieved_docs.content: Physiological Functions: ADH binds to V2 receptors on the distal tubule and collecting ducts of the kidney to up-regulate aquaporin channel expression on the basolateral membrane and increase water reabsorption. It, as its name suggests, also acts as a vasoconstrictor upon binding to V1 receptors on the arteriolar smooth muscle.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Pituitary Hormones -- Mechanism -- Vasopressin
retrieved_docs.content: Two regulating receptors, the subfornical organ and the organum vasculosum in the hypothalamus, sense water deprivation and signal for ADH secretion. [1] A small concentration of vasopressin is sufficient to generate water conservation in the renal tubules. The renal tubules are divided into the proximal, descending, ascending, distal regions, and the collecting duct. The most ADH-dependent segment of the renal tubule is the collecting duct, which has ADH receptors on its basolateral side for ADH to bind and stimulate the Gs protein. The Gs protein stimulates adenylyl cyclase, which further converts ATP into cAMP. High levels of cAMP cause the phosphorylation of protein kinase A, subsequently opening water channels known as aquaporins to allow passage of water from the luminal side to the basolateral side.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Vasopressin -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes insipidus is an important cause of hypernatremia. They are distinguished from each other and primary polydipsia, a disease of dysregulated thirst mechanism resulting in excess fluid intake and, therefore, polydipsia and polyuria, by a water deprivation challenge. In this test, a patient's urine and plasma osmolarity are measured at baseline and then repeatedly measured over a few hours while they are not allowed to drink water. If during this period of water deprivation, their urine osmolarity increases to above 750 mOsm/kg, then primary polydipsia is the diagnosis as this signals the body is adequately releasing ADH in response to a lack of fluid intake. If the urine osmolarity remains low, then this implies an issue with ADH is present, and diabetes insipidus is likely the culprit. To differentiate between nephrogenic and central forms of the disease, during the water deprivation challenge, one may administer desmopressin, an ADH analog. If after desmopressin administration urine osmolarity increases, then central diabetes insipidus is present as this scenario describes a working response ADH. If, however, desmopressin does not increase urine osmolarity, then we know the response to ADH is inappropriate, and it must be nephrogenic diabetes insipidus. This distinction is important to make as the treatment differs between nephrogenic and central diabetes insipidus. The treatment for the central form is to replace the inadequate ADH with desmopressin. In the nephrogenic form, the treatment of choice is thiazide diuretics. Thiazide diuretics act at the distal convoluted tubule to block sodium and chloride cotransport. The increased excretion of sodium chloride induces mild hypovolemia, which triggers increased sodium reabsorption in the proximal convoluted tubule. This increase in sodium reabsorption will promote the increase in passive water reabsorption in the same segment, resulting in a net decrease in water excretion, thus mitigating the polyuria seen in these patients. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Syndrome of Inappropriate Antidiuretic Hormone Secretion -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Vasopressin receptor antagonists such as conivaptan (IV) or tolvaptan (oral) are also available and approved for severe persistent SIADH. These drugs prevent ADH-mediated free water retention by antagonizing V2 receptors and correct hyponatremia. Tolvaptan is hepatotoxic and should not be given to patients with liver disease. Intravenous conivaptan is very effective in correcting hyponatremia and baseline mental status in hospitalized patients. Other therapies, like lithium or demeclocycline, are also effective in SIADH, but both drugs are nephrotoxic and have other potential side effects; therefore, they should only be used when other therapies fail. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2811
retrieved_docs.content: Antagonisté antidiuretického hormonu are categorized as other diuretics in the ATC (Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical) classification system. By blocking the action of antidiuretic hormone, these agents increase water excretion and reduce fluid retention, making them useful in conditions where excess water balance needs to be corrected. Their placement among other diuretics reflects their role in promoting diuresis through a distinct hormonal mechanism rather than the more common electrolyte‑based pathways of classic diuretic classes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 60
retrieved_docs.content: Oxytocin and its analogues are classified as posterior pituitary lobe hormones and are members of the oxytocin family. Vasopressin and its analogues are likewise posterior pituitary lobe hormones, with desmopressin and terlipresin identified as specific members of the vasopressin analogue group. Desmopressin is contraindicated in patients who have اضطراب الكلية (kidney disorder). Recognizing that both oxytocin‑related and vasopressin‑related compounds originate from the posterior pituitary informs clinical decisions about hormone replacement and the safe use of synthetic analogues, particularly avoiding desmopressin in individuals with renal impairment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1929
retrieved_docs.content: Conivaptan, a vasopressin receptor antagonist, exerts a physiologic effect of increased diuresis, meaning that its administration leads to enhanced urine output. This diuretic action of conivaptan can be utilized in clinical situations where promoting fluid excretion is desired, such as in the management of hyponatremia or volume overload. Understanding that increased diuresis is a direct effect of conivaptan helps clinicians anticipate its therapeutic benefits and monitor patients for appropriate fluid balance.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 2499
retrieved_docs.content: Natriuretické látky constitute a class of agents that promote sodium excretion, and within this class the natriuretický peptid typu B is recognized as a specific member. Antagonisté antidiuretického hormonu are considered a subclass of natriuretické látky, reflecting their role as natriuretic agents that block the action of antidiuretic hormone. In the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification system, antagonisté antidiuretického hormonu are further categorized under “other diuretics in ATC,” linking them with other diuretic medications used to manage fluid balance. This hierarchy underscores that ADH antagonists function as natriuretic agents and are grouped with broader diuretic therapies for clinical use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2761
retrieved_docs.content: Antagonisté antidiuretického hormonu, the class of antidiuretic hormone antagonists, includes the drug tolvaptan. Tolvaptan is a selective vasopressin V2‑receptor antagonist that blocks the action of antidiuretic hormone, promoting free water excretion. This pharmacologic class is used in the treatment of conditions such as hyponatremia and autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease, where inhibition of vasopressin‑mediated water reabsorption provides therapeutic benefit.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1578
retrieved_docs.content: Antagonisté antidiuretického hormonu, a class of antidiuretic hormone antagonists, comprises agents such as conivaptan and tolvaptan, both of which are members of this pharmacologic group. This class is categorized within the broader ATC group identified as other diuretics in ATC, linking antidiuretic hormone antagonists to the larger family of diuretic medications. By blocking vasopressin receptors, these agents contribute to the management of disorders associated with water retention and hyponatremia, and their classification alongside other diuretics underscores their role in fluid balance regulation within patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 222
retrieved_docs.content: a potassium‑sparing diuretic can be paired with a loop diuretic (bumetanide) to achieve balanced diuretic therapy while preserving serum potassium levels. Finally, fludrokortison is another member of the Mineralocorticoids for systemic use, plain group, highlighting its role as a synthetic mineralocorticoid used in clinical practice. Together, these entities illustrate the interconnected hierarchy of adrenal hormones, their synthetic analogues, and the pharmacologic agents that modulate their activity, emphasizing the importance of understanding both hormonal pathways and the specific diuretic therapies that target aldosterone‑mediated sodium and potassium balance in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 412
retrieved_docs.content: Desmopressin is contraindicated in patients who have اضطراب الكلية (kidney disorder), indicating that its use should be avoided in individuals with impaired renal function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 339
retrieved_docs.content: Antagonists of antidiuretic hormone constitute a pharmacological group whose molecular mechanisms involve blockade of vasopressin receptors, thereby producing diuretic effects. In the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification system these agents are placed in the “other diuretics” category, reflecting their distinct mode of action compared to traditional diuretics. Tolvaptan is a specific drug that belongs to the class of antagonists of antidiuretic hormone, illustrating that individual agents are members of this broader group. The class also has a parent classification within the larger diuretic hierarchy, linking it to the overall family of diuretic medications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 28
retrieved_docs.content: aquaresis without significant sodium loss. Their molecular mechanism involves selective blockade of V2 receptors in the renal collecting ducts, inhibition of antidiuretic hormone action, reduction of aquaporin‑2 insertion, and consequent aquaretic effect. Conivaptan and tolvaptan are representative agents used to treat hyponatremia and fluid overload in heart‑failure. Together, these relationships illustrate how antimetabolites, gaseous biomarkers, carbon‑based chemical entities, natriuretic peptides, and V2‑receptor antagonists are organized within a structured biomedical framework, emphasizing their interconnections and relevance for diagnosis, therapeutic intervention, and toxicological assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1329
retrieved_docs.content: ADRENAL AND GONADAL HORMONES comprise a family of steroid hormones that includes aldosterone and deoxycorticosterone as subordinate members. Aldosterone functions as a key mineralocorticoid that regulates sodium and water balance, thereby influencing blood pressure and extracellular fluid volume. Deoxycorticosterone serves as a biosynthetic precursor in steroidogenesis and also exhibits mineralocorticoid activity, contributing to electrolyte homeostasis. Recognizing that both aldosterone and deoxycorticosterone are child hormones of the broader ADRENAL AND GONADAL HORMONES group aids clinicians in understanding their integrated roles in endocrine regulation and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2667
retrieved_docs.content: Amiloride is classified within the ATC group “Other potassium‑sparing agents,” and increased diuresis is a physiologic effect of amiloride, reflecting its ability to promote urine output while conserving potassium.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 640
retrieved_docs.content: Vasopressin and its analogues constitute a pharmacologic class that includes both desmopressin and terlipressin, each of which is a member of this group. These agents share structural similarity to vasopressin and act through related mechanisms, making them important therapeutic options within the vasopressin analogue category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2738
retrieved_docs.content: Antagonisté antidiuretického hormonu, which are vasopressin‑type diuretic agents, are categorized in the ATC system under the group “other diuretics.” The drug tolvaptan is a specific member of this class of antidiuretic hormone antagonists.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1416
retrieved_docs.content: PD 134308, atosiban and tolvaptan are all classified as hormone antagonists, with a particular focus on antagonism of antidiuretic hormone (ADH). The class of antidiuretic‑hormone antagonists is itself a subset of the broader hormone‑antagonist group and is categorized within the “other diuretics” segment of the ATC (Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical) classification system. Atosiban, which is derived from the peptide vasotocin, exemplifies a substance that acts through a peptide‑hormone‑receptor antagonist mechanism and therefore belongs to the antidiuretic‑hormone antagonist subclass. Tolvaptan is a member of this antidiuretic‑hormone antagonist class, illustrating that specific drugs can be both individual agents and representatives of the larger pharmacologic group. Collectively, these relationships highlight how PD 134308, atosiban and tolvaptan are interconnected through their shared mechanism of ADH antagonism, their placement within the hormone‑antagonist hierarchy, and their relevance to diuretic therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2594
retrieved_docs.content: The amiloride hydrochloride 5 MG / bumetanide 1 MG oral tablet fulfills the potassium‑sparing diuretic therapeutic role, providing diuretic action while helping to preserve potassium balance in patients who require fluid removal without excessive potassium loss.
